1: “Elihu continued and said.” The formulaic introduction marks the third of Elihu’s four speeches (chs32-33 = first; ch34 = second; ch35 = third; ch36-37 = fourth). Each speech builds on the previous while shifting angle: ch33 addressed the mediation mechanism; ch34 addressed divine justice; ch35 addresses the transcendence argument — whether human righteousness or sin affects God at all. The progression shows Elihu as a systematic theologian working through the implications of his theodicy.
2: “Do you think this is just when you say, ‘My righteousness is more than God’s’?” Mišpāṭ (justice/judgment/right) — the question of what is right/just. Elihu attributes to Job the claim “my righteousness is more than God’s” (ṣiḏqî mēʾēl). Job never said this in these words; the closest approximation is 13:18 (“I know that I will be vindicated”) and 27:2-6 (the righteousness-assertion). Elihu may be extrapolating the logical implication: if God is unjust and Job is just, then Job’s righteousness exceeds God’s justice. Whether that is a fair inference is part of the book’s ongoing argument.
3: “For you say, ‘What does it benefit you? What do I gain from not sinning?’” Mah-yiskōn-lāḵ mah-ʾeoʿîl mēḥaṭṭāʾṯî (what benefit is it to you? what do I gain from not sinning?) — Elihu attributes to Job a calculated cost-benefit analysis of righteousness. This echoes 21:15 (the wicked’s question: “what do we gain by praying to him?”) — Elihu is saying Job has come to reason like the wicked even if unintentionally. The question is the corruption of disinterested piety: Job’s suffering has led him to wonder whether righteousness pays, which Satan predicted from the beginning (1:9-10).
4: “I will give you an answer, and your companions along with you.” ʾAnî ʾᵃšîḇᵉḵā millîn (I will answer you with words) and wᵉʾeṯ-rēʿeḵā ʿimmāḵ (and your companions with you) — Elihu broadens his address: the answer he is about to give concerns not just Job but all the human companions present. The “companions” are presumably the Friends, though they have been silent since ch31. By including them in the address, Elihu signals that his transcendence argument is a universal human concern, not just Job’s private problem.
5: “Look up at the sky and see; behold the clouds, higher than you.” Šûr-šāmayim ûrᵉʾēh (look at the heavens and see) — the imperative to look upward is the beginning of Elihu’s transcendence argument. The physical gesture parallels the whirlwind speeches’ invitation to observe creation (38:4, 18, 33). Wᵉhabbīṭ šeḥāqîm gāḇᵉhû mimmᵉḵā (look at the clouds, they are higher than you) — the incomprehensible height of the heavens above the human being is the spatial image for divine transcendence. What Job says cannot add to or subtract from God; the heavens-gap represents the ontological gap.
6: “If you sin, what do you accomplish against him? If your transgressions multiply, what do you do to him?” Im-ḥāṭāʾtā mah-tipʿal-bô (if you sin, what do you do to him?) — the transcendence argument applied to sin: human transgression does not damage God. Wᵉrabbû pᵉšāʿeḵā mah-tᵃʿᵃśeh-llô (and if your rebellions multiply, what do you do to him?) — the accumulation of human sin does not constitute an assault on God’s being. This is Elihu’s correction of Job’s implicit theology: Job has spoken as though his suffering constitutes an attack by God on someone who can be damaged. But the relationship runs one way ontologically — creatures can be damaged by God but not vice versa.
7: “If you are righteous, what do you give him? What does he receive from your hand?” Im-ṣāḏaqtā mah-tittᵉn-lô (if you are righteous, what do you give him?) — the flip side: human righteousness does not enrich God. Mah-miyyāḏᵉḵā yiqqāḥ (what does he receive from your hand?) — God cannot receive anything from human righteousness that he does not already possess. This is the OT equivalent of Rom 11:35: “who has first given to God, that God should repay him?” The argument cuts against both the Friends’ theology (righteousness deserves reward) and Job’s complaint (God owes Job for his righteousness). Neither transaction is possible with the transcendent God.
8: “Your wickedness harms only someone like yourself, and your righteousness benefits only other people.” Lāʾîš kāmôḵā rišʿeḵā (your wickedness is to a man like yourself) and wᵉlibeni-ʾāḏām ṣiḏqāṯeḵā (and your righteousness to the sons of men) — human moral choices have human consequences, not divine ones. Sin harms the sinner and others in the human community; righteousness benefits others in the human community. God is not the recipient of either. This is both correct and limited as theology: it is true that God does not need human goodness, but Elihu’s argument risks making human moral action completely horizontal, losing the vertical dimension of covenant relationship.
9: “Under the weight of great oppression people cry out; they call for help from the arm of the powerful.” Mērōḇ ʿᵃšûqîm yizʿāqû (because of the multitude of oppressions they cry out) — the social theology of v28 in ch34 reappears. The oppressed cry; this is the normal mechanism that should activate divine justice (Exod 3:7; 22:23). But v10 will note that the cry in v9 is not directed to God. The contrast is between instinctive social-relief seeking (cry to powerful humans) and genuine theological supplication (cry to the God who made you). Elihu is setting up a critique of why some prayers go unanswered: they are cries for human assistance, not genuine address to God.
10: “But no one asks, ‘Where is God my Maker, who gives songs in the night?’” ʾAyyēh ʾĕlôhay ʿōśênî (where is God my Maker) — this is one of the most striking phrases in the Elihu speeches and perhaps in the book. The oppressed cry for human relief rather than seeking “God my Maker.” The personal possessive “my God” and the relational title “my Maker” frame what genuine theological address looks like. The appended phrase nōṯēn zᵉmirôṯ ballāylāh (who gives songs in the night) is unique: God is the source of zᵉmirôṯ (songs, praises) even in the nighttime of suffering. The psalms embody this capacity — to praise in the dark (Ps 42; 77; 88). This is Elihu’s most genuinely pastoral insight.
11: “Who teaches us more than the animals of the earth and makes us wiser than the birds of the sky?” Mᵉlammᵉḏēnû mibbahmôṯ ʾāreṣ (who teaches us beyond the animals of the earth) and ûmēʿôp haššāmayim yᵃḥakkᵉmēnû (and makes us wiser than the birds of the sky) — God’s distinctive gift to humanity over animals is wisdom. The whirlwind speeches will use animals to humble Job (the ostrich, horse, eagle in ch39), but Elihu uses them to honor humanity: God gave humans cognitive capacities animals lack. The implied argument: use those capacities to cry to God rather than to other humans. The wisdom God teaches exceeds animal instinct and should be directed Godward.
12: “There they cry, but he does not answer, because of the pride of evil people.” Šām yiṣʿᵃqû wᵉlōʾ yᵃʿᵃneh (there they cry and he does not answer) — the unanswered cry that Job has experienced and protested. Elihu’s explanation: mippᵉnê gᵉʾôn rāʿîm (because of the pride of evil people). The cry goes unanswered not because God is absent but because the criers are either wicked or crying to the wrong address (humans, v9) or from the wrong posture (pride). This is a partial answer to the theodicy of unanswered prayer — it identifies one category of cases (the proud/wicked who cry for relief without acknowledging God) but does not explain Job’s specific case.
13: “Certainly God does not hear a hollow cry; the Almighty does not regard it.” ʾAḵ-šāwᵉ lōʾ-yišmaʿ ʾēl (surely God does not hear a hollow/empty/vain cry) — šāwᵉ (vain, hollow, empty) is the same root as the third commandment (Exod 20:7: “do not take the name of the LORD in vain”) and Ps 127:1 (“in vain”). A ṣᵃʿāqaṯ-šāwᵉ (hollow cry) is prayer that is form without substance — religious speech that does not actually address the living God. The Almighty does not regard it because it is not genuinely addressed to him. This is Elihu’s most theologically serious critique of a certain type of prayer.
14: “How much less when you say you cannot see him, that the case is before him, and you must wait for him.” ʾAp kî tōʾmar lōʾ tᵉšûrennû (how much less when you say you cannot see him) — Elihu applies the preceding principle to Job’s specific complaint. Job has said he cannot find God to bring his case (23:3-9; 9:11). Dîn lipānāyw (the case/judgment before him) — this phrase concedes that Job’s case does exist before God; it is not ignored. But wᵉḥôlēl lô (you must wait for him) — the appropriate response to a case pending before God is waiting, not impatient accusation. Elihu is recommending the posture of lament psalms: cry, wait, trust that God hears even when silent.
15: “But now, because God has not punished in his anger and does not take sharp notice of every transgression.” Wᵉʿattāh kî-ʾên pāqaḏ ʾappô (and now, because God has not punished in his anger) — Elihu identifies what he sees as Job’s interpretive error: because God has not immediately punished Job’s accusations, Job takes this as license to continue and escalate. Wᵉlōʾ-yāḏaʿ bappaš mᵉʾōḏ (and does not greatly notice transgression) — divine patience is being misread as divine indifference. Job’s continued speech is trading on God’s long-suffering, not recognizing it as grace.
16: “So Job opens his mouth with empty words; he heaps up words without knowledge.” Wᵉʾiyyôḇ hebel yipṣeh-pîhû (Job opens his mouth with hebel) — hebel (breath/vapor/vanity) is Qohelet’s signature word (Eccl 1:2) but here used in its sense of “empty/vain.” Elihu’s final verdict on Job’s speeches in ch35 echoes his verdict in 34:35 (“without knowledge”). The closing charge is exactly the language the divine speeches will open with (38:2). But the context differs entirely: Elihu delivers it as judicial condemnation; God delivers it as the opening move of a transformative encounter. The same words accomplish completely different ends depending on the speaker.
